query_text:
Sequential LLM decoding is slow because each token needs a full
large-model forward pass; how can a small draft model propose
tokens in parallel that the large model verifies without quality
loss?

A.method:
Use a two-model speculative decoding pipeline that preserves the exact output law of the large model (L) while reducing the number of its sequential forward passes. Train a small draft model (D) to approximate L’s next-token distribution on representative data via distillation (cross-entropy on L’s logits) and add a multi-step prediction head that learns to predict K-step lookahead tokens from each state. Calibrate D with temperature and mild label-smoothing so its probabilities are neither over- nor under-confident; maintain a lightweight online calibration layer (e.g., learned affine map on logits) that can be updated from a rolling window of L–D agreement during inference.

At inference, at a given prefix, run D to propose a speculation block: either unroll D for K steps cheaply or use its multi-step head to produce a tree of proposals (depth K, optional small width B). Package all proposed candidate positions into a fork-merge batch for L: reuse the shared prefix KV-cache, then verify the entire proposal block in one or a few L passes by constructing attention masks that branch at the last accepted token and keep branches independent thereafter. This produces L’s logits for every proposed position in parallel.

Perform acceptance-rejection per position to ensure exactness: for the next proposed token y_t from D, accept it with probability a_t = min(1, p_L(y_t|prefix)/p_D(y_t|prefix)). If accepted, proceed to the next proposed token and repeat; once any token is rejected, immediately sample that position from L’s verified distribution (already computed in the batched pass) and discard the remainder of the draft block. The longest run of consecutively accepted tokens becomes the committed extension. Because verification used L’s logits for all candidate positions up to K, there is no extra L pass at the rejection point. Advance the caches to the commit point and start a new speculation block.

Adapt K and the proposal width on-the-fly using a cheap agreement probe: before launching a large speculation block, compare D and L on the next-step distribution for the current prefix using a tiny L probe (e.g., one-step logits only or a cached auxiliary scorer). If the local KL divergence or entropy mismatch is small, increase K (and optionally breadth B); otherwise, shrink K to avoid wasted proposals. Across concurrent requests, microbatch the verification step so L evaluates many proposal trees at once with shared prefix caching; place D on spare GPU/CPU to keep it ahead of L. Fall back to standard decoding when acceptance rate drops below a threshold (e.g., early in a sequence or after topic shifts).

Integrate with both deterministic and stochastic decoding. For greedy decoding, the accept/reject reduces to checking whether D’s argmax token passes the acceptance test; quality remains identical to L’s greedy output. For sampling (temperature, nucleus), draw the global randomness from L’s verified distributions at each accepted or rejected position, using the acceptance-rejection mechanism to thin samples proposed by D. Because accepted tokens are sampled under the exact L distribution and rejected tokens are resampled from L directly at the rejection point, the overall sequence distribution matches L’s, guaranteeing no quality loss.
A.rationale:
This method preserves the exact distribution of the large model by coupling a proposal process (the small model) with an acceptance-rejection correction that uses the large model’s probabilities. By verifying an entire speculative block in a single or few large-model passes with branch-aware attention and shared prefix caches, the approach amortizes L’s expensive computation over multiple tokens, substantially reducing the average number of sequential L steps per generated token.

Training D to match L’s next-token distribution and to predict multiple steps ahead increases acceptance rates, while online calibration and adaptive block sizing prevent wasted proposals when D and L disagree. Microbatching verification across users and placing D on separate compute further increases throughput without affecting correctness. The result is large-model-quality outputs at significantly lower latency and cost.

Because the only tokens that enter the transcript are either (a) accepted under a provably unbiased accept/reject rule using L’s probabilities or (b) drawn directly from L when a proposal is rejected, the final output is distributionally identical to running the large model alone, ensuring no quality loss.

novelty.reasoning: The proposed method combines several known mechanisms: (1) standard speculative decoding with acceptance-rejection (Chen et al. 2023, well-established); (2) distillation-trained draft model (seen in EAGLE, FSPAD, CORAL, etc.); (3) multi-step prediction head (seen in FastMTP, CORAL, multi-token prediction literature); (4) tree-based proposals with shared KV cache (seen in SpecInfer, SuperSpec, EAGLE-2); (5) adaptive speculation length based on acceptance rates (seen in AdaEDL, GammaTune, DASD, Kangaroo); (6) online learning/calibration of draft model during inference (seen in DVI "Draft, Verify, Improve" and OnlineSpec). The most distinctive element is the lightweight online calibration layer (learned affine map on logits) updated from a rolling window of L–D agreement, combined with a KL-divergence probe to adapt K before launching a speculation block. Searches found no paper combining all these elements together — particularly the affine logit calibration layer updated online from rolling L–D agreement. However, individual components (distillation training, multi-step heads, adaptive K, online draft updating) are all present in the literature. The combination of online affine logit calibration + KL probe for adaptive K + distillation + multi-step head is not found as a joint published system, but each mechanism individually is well-covered. This matches the Score 4 anchor: combines mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application for this exact problem, with one named design choice (the online affine logit calibration layer updated from rolling L–D agreement) not present in the surfaced literature.

validity.reasoning: The failure mode in the query is that sequential LLM decoding requires a full large-model forward pass per token, making generation slow. The method directly addresses this by (a) using a small draft model D to propose K tokens cheaply, (b) batching all K proposed positions into a single (or few) large-model forward passes via branch-aware attention and shared KV-cache, and (c) applying the standard acceptance-rejection correction (min(1, p_L/p_D)) to guarantee the output distribution exactly matches L's. The causal chain is fully traceable: draft proposals reduce sequential L passes → batched verification amortizes L's cost over K tokens → acceptance-rejection ensures no quality loss. Additional mechanisms (adaptive K, online calibration, microbatching) further address efficiency without introducing gaps in the core argument. There are no meaningful gaps in the causal pathway from problem to resolution.

coherence.reasoning: The core speculative decoding pipeline is well-specified and internally consistent: draft model training via distillation, batched verification with branch-aware attention masks and shared KV-cache, and the standard acceptance-rejection correction are all compatible mechanisms that an ML practitioner could implement. The adaptive K mechanism introduces a minor coherence tension — it proposes using "a tiny L probe (e.g., one-step logits only or a cached auxiliary scorer)" to estimate KL divergence before launching a speculation block, but this essentially requires an extra L forward pass (or a separate auxiliary model) whose cost and availability are not fully specified, creating a mild circularity. The multi-step prediction head and tree-of-proposals branching are described at a high level but are standard enough that an implementer could fill in the details without fundamental redesign. The online calibration layer updating from a rolling window during inference is plausible but its interaction with the acceptance-rejection guarantee (which assumes D's probabilities are used as-is) is slightly underspecified — if the calibration layer changes D's logits mid-inference, the stored p_D values used in acceptance ratios must be from the post-calibration distribution, which requires careful bookkeeping not explicitly addressed.

B.method:
Build a two-stage speculative decoder around a large verifier L and a small drafter S. Maintain KV caches for both models on the current prefix. At each step, S constructs a short continuation of length K in parallel using two sources: (a) its own fast beam/tree expansion, and (b) a dense, nonparametric continuation retriever. For the retriever, encode the current prefix with L’s last hidden state (already available from prior verification) or S’s last hidden state if L is cold; apply lightweight preprocessing (PCA to low dimension, z-score and Lp normalization), then query an ANN index to fetch a handful of high-likelihood next-token runs. Merge and rank the candidate runs under S to select one draft sequence y1:K; optionally keep a few backup runs for batch verification when GPU is underutilized. 

Verify y1:K with a single forward pass of L over the K proposed tokens, starting from L’s cached prefix state. In deterministic mode (greedy/argmax), accept tokens until the first mismatch with L’s argmax at any position m; append the accepted prefix y1:m to the output, and if a mismatch occurs at m+1, append L’s own token there and discard the rest. In sampling mode, use exact speculative sampling: at each position t, accept y_t with probability min(1, p_L(y_t|prefix+y1:t−1)/q_S(y_t|prefix+y1:t−1)); upon rejection, immediately sample from L’s residual distribution and continue from there. Because all acceptance checks use L’s true probabilities and rejection-correction when sampling, the resulting output exactly preserves L’s distribution. 

Exploit batching and cache sharing to keep verification overhead near-greedy. When there are multiple promising drafts (e.g., top B runs from the retriever or S’s beam), pack them into a batch with shared-prefix masking so the heavy prefix KV state is reused. If batch verification is used, select the longest fully verified run; otherwise fall back to the deterministic or sampling rule above. Overlap stages: while the GPU verifies y1:K for the current prefix, run the CPU or a spare GPU to prefetch dense-retrieval candidates and roll S forward on the hypothesized accepted length, so the next draft is ready immediately after acceptance. 

Raise acceptance rates with lightweight alignment of S to L. Offline, distill S on traces from L with next-token cross-entropy to match L’s local conditionals over common domains. Online, use verifier-in-the-loop updates on cached traffic: reward S proportionally to the length of verified prefixes (and penalize mismatches) with a KL regularizer to its pretrain, nudging S toward proposals L will accept while keeping S small and fast. Combine this with the dense retriever’s filter that prefers runs beginning with L’s current top-1 next token (cheap from the cached last hidden state and LM head). This hybrid “parametric + retrieved” drafting reliably produces long accepted runs without increasing L’s work.
B.rationale:
Speculative decoding accelerates generation by shifting most steps from expensive verification to cheap drafting, and then confirming multiple tokens in one pass. The proposed pipeline preserves quality because the large model L remains the arbiter: in greedy decoding, only tokens that match L’s argmax are emitted; in sampling, rejection-corrected acceptance ensures the exact L distribution. Thus, there is no degradation relative to running L alone. 

Dense retrieval and drafter alignment specifically target the key bottleneck—acceptance length. Semantic retrieval provides high-precision multi-token candidates tailored to the current context, and aligning S to L’s local conditionals further reduces mismatches. Batched, shared-prefix verification and pipelined compute turn these longer accepted runs into real wall-clock gains, yielding the intended parallelism from S without extra errors.

inspired_by.borrowed_aspect: dense semantic retrieval for multi-token drafts and exact, batched speculative verification
proposal–verification framing with rejection-corrected acceptance to preserve target distribution
verifier-in-the-loop, step-wise reward shaping to train the drafter for locally verifiable actions

novelty.reasoning: The proposed method is essentially a combination of: (1) DReSD's dense retrieval using L's hidden states with PCA/dual-normalization/ANN for speculative drafting — this is nearly identical to DReSD (seed 2); (2) online RL-based alignment of the draft model using accepted-run-length reward + KL regularizer — this is closely matched by DVI (KL→RL schedule with verifier accept/reject signals) and Online Speculative Decoding; (3) batched shared-prefix verification and pipelined CPU prefetch — these are engineering optimizations present in various speculative decoding papers. The proposed method adds a "parametric + retrieved" hybrid drafting (S's beam/tree expansion combined with retrieval), but this combination of a neural drafter plus retrieval-based drafting is a known pattern in speculative decoding. No single paper combines all three mechanisms (DReSD-style dense retrieval + online RL alignment with KL regularizer + hybrid parametric/retrieved drafting), but each pairing is covered: DReSD covers (1), DVI covers (2), and the hybrid drafting is a straightforward extension. This matches the Score 3 anchor — combining 2-3 known mechanisms where searches surface papers pairing the same mechanisms for closely related problems, with no single paper combining all three but each sub-combination being well-covered.

validity.reasoning: The failure mode in the query is that sequential LLM decoding requires a full large-model forward pass per token, making generation slow. The method directly addresses this by using a small drafter S to propose K tokens in parallel, then verifying them in a single forward pass of L — the canonical speculative decoding mechanism. The method further elaborates on acceptance criteria (both greedy and sampling modes with rejection correction) that preserve L's exact output distribution, directly addressing the "without quality loss" constraint. Additional components (retrieval-augmented drafting, KV cache sharing, batched verification, drafter alignment) all causally connect to improving acceptance rates and reducing wall-clock time. The causal chain from problem (sequential full-pass decoding) to solution (parallel draft + single-pass batch verification) is fully traceable with no significant gaps.

coherence.reasoning: The method is broadly coherent and combines well-established components (speculative decoding, KV caching, ANN retrieval, distillation, online RL-style updates) in a compatible way. The core speculative decoding loop — draft with S, verify with L in one forward pass, accept/reject with exact probability correction — is standard and correctly described. The main coherence weaknesses are: (1) the "merge and rank candidate runs under S" step from heterogeneous sources (beam expansion + retrieval) is under-specified — how exactly token-run probabilities from the retriever are scored under S's distribution is not detailed; (2) the batch verification with "select the longest fully verified run" mixes deterministic and sampling acceptance in a way that could subtly break the exact-distribution guarantee unless carefully handled (the method doesn't fully specify how rejection sampling interacts with multiple candidate sequences); (3) the online verifier-in-the-loop update with a KL regularizer is mentioned but the training loop mechanics (when updates fire, how cached traffic is stored, gradient flow) are vague enough that an implementer would need to make significant design choices. These are notable gaps but not fundamental contradictions — a skilled ML practitioner could fill them with reasonable choices without redesigning the core pipeline.

C.method:
We propose Parallel Draft-and-Verify (PDV) decoding. A small draft model D is trained to predict a block of the next B tokens in parallel given the current prefix. Architecturally, D uses a two-stream, position-specific masked attention decoder so that every target position in the upcoming block has its own query stream while sharing a common context stream built from the prefix; this enables simultaneous next-B predictions without autoregressive recurrence. At inference, D emits either a single top-1 block or K multi-view blocks (multi-hypothesis heads) in one forward pass.

The large model L does not decode these tokens sequentially. Instead, it performs a single, cached forward pass per candidate block by running on the concatenation of the prefix and the block under the standard causal mask, producing logits at all new positions simultaneously. For multiple candidate blocks, we pack them into one batched pass (or a block-diagonal mask) that reuses the prefix KV-cache, so the incremental cost scales primarily with B and the number of packed candidates, not with the number of tokens times L’s depth.

We then apply exact acceptance-correction left-to-right within each candidate block using L’s logits to preserve L’s sampling distribution without quality loss. For position t in the block, compute pL = softmaxL(· | accepted prefix so far) and qD = softmaxD(· | original prefix) at that position. Accept the drafted token y with probability min(1, pL(y)/qD(y)); upon the first rejection in a block, resample that position directly from pL and discard all later drafted tokens in that block. Commit all accepted tokens to the prefix and repeat: D proposes the next block conditioned on the new prefix, L verifies in one pass, and acceptance-correction ensures the overall output is exactly distributed as if L had decoded sequentially.

To raise acceptance and reduce L’s workload further, D is distilled from L with a multi-token objective: given a prefix, predict the next B tokens with teacher-forced targets from L (and optionally multiple temperatures) so qD closely matches pL. Multi-view heads in D provide diverse yet high-probability alternatives per position; we pack a small number of these blocks per iteration and verify them together, selecting the first block that fully accepts the longest prefix or, failing that, falling back to L’s resample at the earliest mismatch. KV-caching for the prefix is persistent across iterations, and verification computes logits only once per block, yielding near-B× speedups when acceptance is high while remaining exact by design.
C.rationale:
The bottleneck in autoregressive decoding is that each next token requires a full forward pass of the large model. PDV shifts the heavy, speculative expansion to a cheap draft model that can propose many tokens at once, and amortizes the large model’s computation by verifying an entire block with a single cached pass. The acceptance-correction step guarantees that, regardless of the draft model’s quality, the emitted sequence is distributed identically to the large model’s sequential decoder, so there is no quality loss.

Two elements make this efficient: (i) a parallel block-prediction draft model that avoids token-by-token recurrence, and (ii) a verification procedure that evaluates all positions in the proposed block at once while maintaining causal correctness via left-to-right acceptance. When the draft model is well-matched to the large model (via distillation) the acceptance rate is high, so each large-model pass commits many tokens; even with occasional rejections, overall latency drops substantially without altering the large model or its distribution.

inspired_by.borrowed_aspect: two-stream masked attention to enable position-wise parallel predictions
cached-trajectory parallel evaluation idea (Picard-style sweeps over time with proposals)
multi-view heads to produce diverse parallel candidates while remaining ANN-friendly

C.paraphrase_essence: Sequential single-step inference incurs O(N) expensive forward passes when N items must be decided in order, even when many decisions could be made in parallel.

novelty.reasoning: The literature search confirms that parallel/non-autoregressive draft models for speculative decoding are well-established (PARD, DFlash, SpecFormer all generate multiple tokens in one draft forward pass). Multiple-hypothesis drafting also exists (Medusa uses multiple heads; tree-based verification is standard in EAGLE). The standard acceptance-rejection correction for speculative decoding is the original Chen et al./Leviathan et al. framework. Distillation of draft models from the target LLM is also established (EAGLE series). What PDV adds that is not directly surfaced is the specific two-stream position-specific masked attention architecture (borrowed from RetroMAE) applied as the draft model architecture, combined with multi-view hypothesis heads packed into a single block-diagonal batched verification pass. No search result surfaces a paper that combines a two-stream position-specific masked attention draft model with multi-hypothesis block packing for speculative decoding. However, the individual components (parallel draft, multi-head drafting, distillation, acceptance-correction) are all well-known, and the two-stream architecture is a known design from retrieval pre-training applied here as a new architectural choice for the draft model. This matches the Score 4 anchor: combines mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application for this problem, introducing one named design choice (two-stream position-specific masked attention draft model + multi-view block packing) not present in the surfaced literature, while the broader framework of parallel speculative decoding with distillation is established.

validity.reasoning: (a) The failure mode is sequential LLM decoding requiring one full large-model forward pass per token, making generation slow. (b) The method proposes a parallel block-prediction draft model D that generates B tokens simultaneously using a two-stream masked attention architecture, followed by a single batched forward pass of the large model L over the entire candidate block using KV-cache reuse, and applies speculative decoding's exact acceptance-correction to preserve L's output distribution. (c) The mechanism directly and causally engages every aspect of the failure mode: the draft model eliminates per-token recurrence in proposal generation, the single cached forward pass of L amortizes verification cost across B tokens, and the acceptance-correction step with mathematical guarantees (min(1, pL/qD) acceptance probability) explicitly resolves the quality-loss concern. The causal chain from problem to solution is fully traceable with no gaps.

coherence.reasoning: The method is a well-specified extension of standard speculative decoding (Leviathan et al., Chen et al.) with clearly described components: a parallel draft model using two-stream masked attention, a single batched verification pass reusing KV-cache, and exact acceptance-correction. The acceptance-correction procedure is correctly described and is a known valid technique for preserving the target distribution. The main coherence concern is the two-stream position-specific masked attention architecture for D — while inspired by XLNet-style two-stream attention, the exact masking scheme needed to predict B tokens simultaneously without any autoregressive dependency among them (yet conditioned on the prefix) requires careful specification that is somewhat underspecified here, but not fatally so. The multi-hypothesis head selection logic ("selecting the first block that fully accepts the longest prefix") is slightly ambiguous when multiple blocks partially accept, but an implementer could resolve this with a straightforward design choice. Overall the components are compatible and the method is implementable with minor design decisions.

D.method:
We build a propose–verify cascade with a small draft model D and the target large model L. D shares the tokenizer with L and is equipped with H parallel, depth-specialized next-token heads (one for each lookahead position 1..H). At each decoding step, D proposes a compact speculative tree: a main path of length up to H plus a small number of high-confidence alternatives per depth. A lightweight uncertainty head attached to D prunes low-confidence branches and orders candidates, so the verification workload focuses on proposals most likely to be accepted. The proposals are then packed into a prefix-sharing trie and sent to L in a single batched verification pass that reuses KV caches for shared prefixes.

D is trained with verifier-aware distillation from L. Using unlabeled text (and optionally prompts from deployment domains), we collect prefixes, query L once to obtain its next-token distribution and top-k candidates at each future position, and optimize D’s shared backbone plus depth-specialized heads to match L via KL on teacher-forced continuations. In parallel, we supervise the uncertainty head to predict per-candidate acceptance likelihood under the exact speculative acceptance rule (defined below), turning L’s responses into pseudo targets for when and where D should speculate. This yields heads specialized for different lookahead positions and an explicit confidence signal that controls tree width at inference.

Inference uses exact speculative sampling to preserve L’s quality. We first sample a main path from D. L then verifies the packed tree: for each step i along the main path, we accept the token with probability min(1, p_L(x_i|ctx)/p_D(x_i|ctx)), and if rejected we draw a correction token from the residual r ∝ p_L − min(p_L, p_D), append it, and resume speculation from the new context. This acceptance–correction procedure guarantees the final sample is distributed exactly as if decoded from L alone, with no quality loss. To maximize throughput, we verify multiple proposed depths and alternatives in one L forward by packing the trie and caching shared keys/values; accepted prefixes are committed atomically, and any divergence triggers a single-token correction before continuing.

A lightweight controller adapts speculation depth H and branch budget online. It maintains exponential moving averages of acceptance rate, correction frequency, and verify-pass latency. When acceptance is high and verification remains the bottleneck, it grows H or adds one alternative at early depths; when acceptance drops or corrections spike, it shrinks H and prunes branches using the uncertainty head. This keeps GPU utilization high while avoiding wasted verification on unlikely candidates. The result is a drop-in decoding mode that delivers substantial tokens-per-second gains with exact-match distributional fidelity to L.
D.rationale:
The cascade lets a small model generate many future tokens in parallel while the large model does only what it must: a single, cache-efficient verification of a compact proposal set. Depth-specialized draft heads increase the chance that early lookahead positions match the large model, while the uncertainty head concentrates compute on likely-accepted paths. The exact acceptance–correction rule preserves L’s sampling distribution, so speed-ups incur no quality loss. An adaptive controller stabilizes performance across prompts and temperatures by tracking acceptance statistics and tuning speculation depth and branching online.

Together, these pieces turn sequential decoding into a propose-once, verify-once loop per chunk: D parallelizes proposal, L amortizes verification over a packed trie, and the acceptance–correction step guarantees equivalence to L-only decoding.

inspired_by.borrowed_aspect: lightweight selector followed by heavyweight reasoner cascade
hierarchical specialization to different submodules (depth-specific heads)
single-pass uncertainty head via prototype-based latent scoring
EMA-based online adaptivity controller for noise/acceptance statistics
using pseudo targets when ground truth is unavailable (teacher-driven supervision)
avoid self-evaluation bias by separating proposer and verifier roles

novelty.reasoning: The proposed method combines: (1) depth-specialized parallel draft heads (similar to Medusa), (2) an explicit uncertainty head trained to predict per-candidate acceptance probability under the exact speculative acceptance rule (a specific novel training signal not found in any searched paper), (3) trie-based prefix-sharing verification (known from SpecInfer/beam search work), (4) verifier-aware KL distillation of draft heads from the target model, and (5) an online adaptive controller using EMA of acceptance/correction statistics to tune tree depth and branch budget. Searches surfaced Medusa (multiple heads + tree attention), OPT-Tree (adaptive tree structure), PCT (confidence-based pruning), AdaEDL/SVIP (entropy-based adaptive draft length), and LK Losses (direct acceptance rate optimization). However, no paper was found that combines depth-specialized heads with an explicitly trained uncertainty head that predicts per-candidate acceptance probability under the exact speculative acceptance rule, plus online EMA-based adaptive depth/width control. The closest is Medusa + OPT-Tree + AdaEDL combined, but no single paper unifies all these mechanisms. The uncertainty head trained on pseudo-targets derived from the exact acceptance rule is a named novel component not present in any surfaced paper. This matches the Score 4 anchor: combines mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application, introducing one named design choice (the acceptance-probability-predicting uncertainty head) not in the surfaced literature.

validity.reasoning: The failure mode in the query is sequential decoding requiring a full large-model forward pass per token, causing slowness. The method directly addresses this by (a) using a small draft model D with depth-specialized heads to propose multiple tokens in parallel, (b) packing proposals into a prefix-sharing trie for a single batched verification pass by L, and (c) applying exact speculative sampling (accept/reject with residual correction) to guarantee distributional equivalence to L-only decoding. The causal chain is fully traceable: parallel proposal replaces sequential large-model passes, batched trie verification amortizes L's cost, and the acceptance-correction rule closes the quality-loss gap. The adaptive controller further tightens the mechanism by dynamically tuning speculation depth to maintain throughput. There are no meaningful gaps in the causal pathway from problem to resolution.

coherence.reasoning: The method is a well-structured extension of speculative decoding with tree/trie verification, depth-specialized heads, and an uncertainty-guided pruning mechanism. The core speculative sampling acceptance-correction rule is standard and correctly stated. The main coherence concerns are: (1) the "depth-specialized heads" on D require the shared backbone to produce representations useful for multi-step lookahead, but the training procedure uses teacher-forced continuations from L, which is a reasonable if slightly under-specified setup — an implementer could fill this in; (2) the uncertainty head is trained to predict per-candidate acceptance likelihood under the exact speculative rule, but the pseudo-targets for this require knowing p_L and p_D at inference time during training data collection, which is feasible but adds a non-trivial data pipeline step that is glossed over; (3) the trie packing with KV cache reuse for shared prefixes is a known technique (e.g., from SpecInfer/Medusa) and is compatible with the rest of the method. None of these gaps are blocking — an ML practitioner familiar with speculative decoding could implement this with reasonable design choices. The adaptive controller is straightforward given the described statistics. Overall the method is mostly coherent with minor under-specification in the training data pipeline and head architecture details.

query_text:
VLM inference latency scales with visual token count but most
tokens are redundant; how can we identify and remove or merge
low-information visual tokens at inference without retraining
the encoder?

A.method:
We propose Prompt-Conditioned Layerwise Token Pooling (PCLTP), an inference-only procedure that constructs a sparse pooling matrix to prune or merge visual tokens before they enter the decoder’s cross-attention, without changing any encoder or decoder weights. Given an image, we run the frozen vision encoder once to obtain T visual tokens and their positions. We then build a token-importance score that is conditioned on the current text prompt and a redundancy model over the token embeddings. Finally, we aggregate tokens using a row-stochastic pooling matrix G ∈ R^{T×M} (M << T) and feed the pooled representation to the language model. All operations are linear and take place outside training.

To compute importance, we use a cheap prompt-conditioned probe and two redundancy-aware signals. First, run a text-only forward pass through the first L_p decoder layers to obtain query vectors Q_l for each head. In parallel, linearly project the visual tokens once through each cross-attention layer’s key matrix W_k^l to get K_l (no gradient). The text-conditioned saliency for token t is s_text(t) = Σ_l,heads softmax(Q_l K_l^T)_t, optionally averaged over a few early layers. Second, estimate redundancy via cosine similarity of token embeddings within local spatial neighborhoods (e.g., k-NN over a 2D grid) to measure how replaceable a token is by its neighbors. Third, add a content-strength prior such as token embedding norm or local image-edge density aligned to the token’s patch. The final score is S(t) = α·s_text(t) + β·strength(t) − γ·redundancy(t). Keep the top-K tokens as protected singletons.

For the remaining tokens, perform constrained agglomerative merging within overlapping windows to reach a target M. Candidate merges are chosen to minimize a distortion objective that is linear in attention space: for a candidate cluster C, minimize E(C) = Σ_l [Σ_{t∈C} ||K_l(t) − K̄_l(C)||^2 + λ||V_l(t) − V̄_l(C)||^2], where V_l = W_v^l·tokens and bars denote weighted means using S(t) as weights. This yields per-cluster weights that define a convex combination; stacking these weights forms the pooling matrix G (rows sum to 1, nonnegative), with protected tokens mapped one-to-one. This step ensures merges are low-error simultaneously for both keys and values across layers, preserves spatial coherence, and prevents crossing strong appearance boundaries.

Finally, precompute per-layer keys and values for all T tokens once, then pool them using the same G: K'_l = G^T K_l and V'_l = G^T V_l for every cross-attention layer l. The decoder attends over M pooled memories instead of T originals at every generation step, cutting both compute and memory by ≈T/M. Optional elastic refinement: during the first H generated words, monitor output entropy and attention mass on pooled groups; if uncertainty is high and concentrates on a merged group, regenerate K'_l,V'_l with a less aggressive M and continue decoding from the next step (text KV caches are retained; visual KV is replaced), bounding fallback overhead. This yields a single-pass speedup with a small, amortized precompute and no retraining.
A.rationale:
This method targets the true bottleneck—cross-attention over many static visual tokens—by reducing the number of keys/values seen by the language model, not by altering model parameters. Prompt-conditioning ensures that tokens relevant to the current query are preserved while uninformative or redundant regions are merged. Because attention is linear in keys/values, pooling directly in KV space preserves the model’s computations more faithfully than manipulating embeddings alone; the distortion-controlled clustering further bounds approximation error across all layers.

The cost of computing per-layer K/V once and building G is small compared to repeatedly attending to T tokens over dozens of decoding steps, so speedups scale with sequence length and number of layers. Constraining merges to be convex combinations, protecting top-K salient tokens, and allowing a lightweight early refinement safeguard quality without retraining the encoder or decoder. The result is a practical, drop-in inference-time compression that prunes or merges low-information visual tokens and lowers latency proportional to the compression ratio.

However, none of these papers combine: (1) running a partial decoder forward pass to obtain query vectors for prompt-conditioned saliency scoring, (2) agglomerative merging that minimizes a distortion objective simultaneously over both keys AND values across multiple layers, (3) a row-stochastic pooling matrix G applied uniformly to precomputed per-layer KV pairs, and (4) elastic refinement during decoding based on output entropy. The closest is AdaptInfer (text-guided scoring) + WeightedKV (KV merging via convex combinations), but no paper combines these specific mechanisms together for this problem. The KV-space distortion-minimizing agglomerative merge with a pooling matrix applied across all layers simultaneously is a named design choice not present in any surfaced paper. This matches the Score 4 anchor: combines mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application, introducing specific new components (the multi-layer KV distortion objective for agglomerative merging, the partial-decoder-pass saliency, and the elastic refinement) not found together in the literature.

validity.reasoning: (a) The failure mode is that VLM inference latency scales with visual token count because most tokens are redundant, and the goal is to identify and remove/merge low-information tokens at inference without retraining. (b) The method constructs a prompt-conditioned importance score combining text-query saliency, redundancy, and content strength, then performs agglomerative merging in KV space to reduce T tokens to M tokens, pooling precomputed keys and values so the decoder attends over M instead of T memories — all without modifying encoder or decoder weights. (c) The mechanism directly engages every aspect of the failure mode: redundancy is explicitly modeled and penalized in the scoring function, prompt-conditioning ensures task-relevant tokens are preserved, the KV-space pooling directly reduces the cross-attention cost that causes latency scaling, and the entire procedure is inference-only with frozen weights. The causal chain from problem (redundant tokens → latency) to solution (score → merge → pooled KV → fewer attention operations) is fully traceable with no significant gaps.

coherence.reasoning: The method is broadly coherent and well-specified. The core pipeline — frozen encoder → importance scoring → agglomerative merging → KV pooling — is internally consistent and each step is implementable. The main coherence tension is in the importance scoring step: computing s_text(t) requires a "text-only forward pass through the first L_p decoder layers to obtain query vectors Q_l," but in a standard VLM cross-attention setup, the text queries at inference time depend on previously generated tokens, meaning this partial forward pass is somewhat underspecified (which text tokens? the prompt only? how are positional embeddings handled without visual context?). This is a real but not fatal gap — a practitioner could make reasonable choices. The elastic refinement step (replacing visual KV mid-generation) is described at a high level and assumes text KV caches remain valid after visual KV replacement, which holds if cross-attention and self-attention caches are separate, but this architectural assumption is not universally true and is not explicitly justified. The distortion objective and pooling matrix construction are well-defined and consistent. Overall these are fillable gaps rather than blocking contradictions.

B.method:
SIEVE (Semantic-Head-Guided Inference-time Visual Token Selection and Merging) is a training-free procedure that reduces visual tokens fed to a VLM while preserving answer-critical content. Given a frozen vision encoder and a text prompt/question, we first run a cheap "probe" step that computes query-conditioned relevance for each visual token using the LLM’s existing attentions. Specifically, we identify semantic attention heads (those that naturally emphasize visual/semantic tokens in higher layers) and aggregate the self-/cross-attention from the last few text positions onto visual tokens to obtain a per-token relevance score. In parallel, we measure token redundancy via local cosine similarity in key/value space and spatial proximity on the patch grid. A combined score g_i = α·relevance_i − β·redundancy_i + γ·norm_i ranks tokens, and a coverage-aware top-K (k-means++/grid quota) forms the keep set S_keep.

Tokens not in S_keep are merged into their nearest kept neighbor to preserve information without increasing sequence length. For each kept token j, we pool the value/key vectors of its assigned neighbors with weights proportional to their similarity and (optionally) their query-conditioned attention, replacing j’s embedding with the pooled result and removing the neighbors from the sequence. To keep the language-side positional expectations stable despite removing tokens, we preserve original visual token absolute indices for RoPE (by applying a base offset so text tokens begin at the same phase they would with full-length vision) or, where per-token positions are not exposed, we maintain “ghost” position counters without attending to actual dropped tokens.

During generation, SIEVE operates progressively. Decoding starts with a small S_keep; at each step we estimate the model’s current visual dependency using a PDM-like delta: we momentarily mask vision contributions in the KV-cache for the next-token prediction and measure the divergence of logits from the vision-conditioned distribution. If the dependency drops below a threshold, we unmerge and admit the next tranche of tokens (highest held-out g_i, ensuring new spatial coverage), update their KV entries only in later layers to limit cost, and continue decoding. In parallel, a token-wise saturation check monitors hidden-state similarity across adjacent layers; tokens whose representations have converged (high cosine similarity across layers or to their regional centroid) remain merged, while unsaturated ones are candidates for restoration.

SIEVE is plug-in and architecture-agnostic: for concat-style LVLMs (e.g., LLaVA) we use text-to-vision self-attention; for cross-attention decoders we use cross-attention weights. It requires no retraining, only read-access to attentions/hidden states and the ability to set per-token positions (or a RoPE offset). In typical 14×14–24×24 patch settings, SIEVE retains 20–40% of tokens initially and admits more only when the PDM gate signals need, yielding substantial LLM-side latency reductions while preserving accuracy.
B.rationale:
Low-information tokens can be identified at inference by how much the model’s text stream actually attends to them and by how redundant they are with neighbors. Using semantic heads focuses the scoring on pathways that already carry visual semantics, so the keep set preserves the information flow the frozen model relies on. Merging discarded tokens into nearby kept tokens compresses detail without discarding regional evidence, and maintaining effective positional phases prevents distribution shift when the text attends over a shortened visual prefix.

The progressive controller protects quality: when the model’s reliance on vision weakens (as detected by a PDM-style measure) or when hidden states indicate unmet detail (insufficient saturation), SIEVE selectively restores tokens, mirroring staged integration. This balances speed and accuracy per example without retraining the encoder.

The PDM-style delta requiring a "momentary masking of vision contributions in the KV-cache" at each decoding step adds a non-trivial overhead and the exact mechanism for doing this without recomputation is not explained. C.method:
We propose VIS-ZIP, an inference-time, plug-in compressor that sits between a frozen visual encoder and the VLM decoder. VIS-ZIP identifies redundant visual tokens and either removes them or merges them into a much smaller set of surrogate tokens (centroids) without retraining. The pipeline has three stages: (1) a short, budgeted probing phase to estimate per-token influence on the decoder; (2) coverage-aware grouping and merging of similar tokens into centroids with proper rescaling; and (3) an adaptive control loop during generation that monitors cross-attention and reversibly splits centroids on demand.

1) Probing and importance scoring. Given encoder outputs (keys K, values V, and positions) for N visual tokens, we run a small number of probe steps (e.g., the instruction and 1–2 initial answer tokens) with full tokens cached. We compute a saliency score s_i per token by combining cross-attention usage and value content across heads/layers: s_i = sum_over_probe_steps,sum_over_heads ||A_{t,h,i}||1 * ||V_{h,i}||2. To prevent overestimating tokens that are attended due to language prior rather than visual necessity, we apply a conditioning-dependence test on each probe step: we compare the next-token distribution with and without the image (or with image masked to a uniform prior) using a distance such as Hellinger; tokens whose removal causes negligible change are down-weighted. A single backward pass can optionally refine s_i via gradient·input saliency on V_i to capture first-order influence at low overhead.

2) Coverage-aware merging with rescaling. We form a k-NN graph over K (fast ANN such as HNSW) and cluster tokens with low K-distance while enforcing spatial/appearance coverage (e.g., limit cluster diameter and keep at least one representative per coarse grid cell). For each cluster c, we produce merged keys/values as K_c = sum_i w_i K_i and V_c = sum_i w_i V_i with weights w_i ∝ s_i (normalized within the cluster) to bias preservation toward influential members. To maintain the dot-product scale seen during training, we rescale merged keys/values following attention decoupling principles: divide K_c and V_c by sqrt(sum_i w_i^2) so that E[Q·K_c] and the magnitude of the attended V_c remain comparable to the unmerged case. The result is M ≪ N surrogate tokens that replace the full set in cross-attention. If the VLM expects fixed image-length prompts, we retain placeholder “ghost” tokens in the original positions with zeroed values so positional structure is preserved while compute follows the compressed set.

3) Adaptive generation with reversible splits. We start decoding with the M-centroid set and monitor per-step cross-attention. If a centroid accumulates high attention while its intra-cluster K variance is large or if the output distribution under centroids deviates from the full-cache baseline above a small threshold (measured on the initial probe or via periodic one-step checks), we split that centroid into its constituent subclusters (or top-ranked members by s_i) and continue. Conversely, if attention collapses onto a few centroids for several steps, we can further merge low-use centroids to shrink M. For extreme budgets, we offer a rank-r alternative: build a low-rank basis of K and V by truncated SVD over the token matrices and instantiate r pseudo-tokens (basis vectors) that act like visual prefix vectors; this preserves the principal subspace influencing cross-attention without any training. Throughout, we keep the original K,V in cache so expansions are O(cluster_size) and amortized rare.

Practically, VIS-ZIP is a drop-in function: compute encoder once; run 1–2 probe steps to score tokens; pick a target retention M by increasing compression until the probe distribution-distance stays below ε; cluster and merge with rescaling; decode with adaptive split checks. Complexity per decoding step drops from O(N) to O(M) in cross-attention, with one-time O(N log N) clustering and occasional O(cluster_size) splits.
C.rationale:
The decoder’s cross-attention cost scales linearly with the number of visual tokens at every step, yet many tokens are semantically redundant or carry negligible mass under the decoder’s queries. VIS-ZIP first quantifies which tokens actually influence the next-token distribution (not merely attract attention due to language priors) and then replaces groups of similar, low-influence tokens with properly rescaled centroids so that the decoder sees near-identical dot-product statistics. The adaptive split mechanism guarantees that if a merged region becomes important later in generation (e.g., when the question turns to a small detail), the system can restore fine-grained tokens for just that region, keeping quality while preserving most of the speedup.

This design requires no retraining of the visual encoder or the VLM: it only manipulates K,V produced by the frozen encoder. The probing-based dependency check bounds quality regressions; coverage constraints prevent losing small but important regions; key/value rescaling preserves attention magnitudes seen at train time; and placeholder tokens (when needed) maintain positional structure. Together, these choices deliver substantial token-count reductions—and thus latency savings—while keeping the model’s semantics intact.

C.paraphrase_essence: Inference cost scales with the number of input units even though many are low-information, and with a frozen encoder there is no safe inference-time mechanism to discard or consolidate them without risking quality loss.

(b) The method proposes a three-stage pipeline: probing to score per-token influence on the decoder, coverage-aware clustering and merging of similar tokens into centroids with attention-scale-preserving rescaling, and an adaptive split/merge loop during generation — all operating on frozen encoder outputs. (c) Each stage directly engages the failure mode: the probing stage identifies which tokens are genuinely influential (not just attended due to language priors), the merging stage reduces N to M tokens while preserving dot-product statistics so the frozen decoder sees comparable attention magnitudes, and the adaptive splitting stage handles the edge case where merged regions become important mid-generation. coherence.reasoning: The method is broadly coherent and well-specified across its three stages. The saliency scoring, clustering, and merging steps are all grounded in standard ML primitives (attention weights, ANN indexing, weighted averaging, SVD). The rescaling heuristic (dividing by sqrt(sum w_i^2)) is a reasonable approximation but is not rigorously derived — it assumes independence and linearity that may not hold in softmax attention, and the "attention decoupling principles" cited are vague. The adaptive split mechanism is implementable but the threshold ε for triggering splits requires careful calibration that is under-specified. The "ghost token" placeholder approach for fixed-length VLMs introduces a subtle inconsistency: if ghost tokens have zeroed values but non-zero keys, they still participate in softmax normalization and could distort attention distributions; if both K and V are zeroed, positional embeddings may still interfere. These are non-trivial but not blocking issues — an ML practitioner could make reasonable design choices to resolve them without fundamental redesign.

D.method:
We propose a plug-and-play inference-time reducer that sits between the frozen visual encoder and the language model. Given an input image (or frames) and a text prompt, we first run the frozen visual encoder exactly as-is to obtain the dense set of visual tokens. We then compute a composite importance score per token from three cheap signals: (1) intra-image uniqueness, (2) prompt-conditioned relevance, and (3) background-prototype similarity. Finally, we prune the lowest-scoring tokens and merge the rest of the low-importance ones into a compact set of supertokens, preserving keys/values and positional statistics so the downstream cross-attention sees a semantically faithful but much shorter token sequence. No encoder weights are touched; all scoring, pruning, and merging occur on the encoder outputs.

Uniqueness is computed by an intra-image dedup measure inspired by set-difference metrics: for each token embedding, we estimate how much novel content it contributes relative to its nearest neighbors. Concretely, we approximate each token’s redundancy as the maximum cosine similarity to any other token in a local spatial-similarity graph (built via kNN in embedding space with spatial adjacency as a prior), and define uniqueness as one minus this redundancy. Prompt-conditioned relevance is computed by a lightweight cross-modal probe: we embed the text prompt with a frozen text encoder (e.g., the same VL projector or a small CLIP-like text tower if available) and score each visual token by its dot-product similarity to the prompt embedding or to a handful of noun-phrase embeddings extracted from the prompt. Background-prototype similarity leverages a small, precomputed codebook of "background" prototypes (textures, skies, walls, road, grass, etc.) obtained once by running k-means over frozen visual-token embeddings from a background-heavy corpus; tokens that strongly match these prototypes are down-weighted unless they are also highly prompt-relevant. The final importance score is an adaptive blend of these three signals, and we target a token budget by thresholding this score.

Given scores, we keep the top-M tokens intact (highest importance) and perform structure-preserving merging over the remainder. We cluster the low-importance tokens using embedding similarity gated by spatial proximity and iteratively merge until we meet a target budget B. For each cluster, we create a supertoken whose key/value vectors are the weighted average of member tokens’ keys/values (weights proportional to importance or to inverse redundancy), and whose position is the centroid of member positions (optionally preserving a small positional covariance for rotary/relative encodings via averaging angles or keeping the dominant orientation). The merged token set replaces the original tokens in the cross-attention layers of the language model; text-side states and attention masks are unchanged. This merging preserves aggregate evidence while slashing token count, so latency falls roughly in proportion to the reduction.

To recover rare-but-important details with minimal overhead, we add an optional two-pass pseudo-relevance feedback. First, run a very low-budget pass (e.g., B/3 tokens) to obtain a quick sketch: extract top attended visual tokens from the model’s first cross-attention or the nouns/entities mentioned in the preliminary text output. Use this as feedback to upweight tokens that lie near those attended regions/entities and re-run the reducer to produce the final budget B. Because text-side computations can be cached and only the cross-modal attention scales with visual tokens, this refinement adds little latency but substantially improves recall of small, salient regions.

For stability across inputs, we adapt the pruning threshold online. We keep an exponential moving average of the distribution of uniqueness, relevance, and prototype scores, and adjust blending weights and the pruning cutoff to hit a target budget while avoiding aggressive drops on high-entropy scenes. For efficiency, we compute token neighbors with approximate kNN, limit pairwise similarities to local windows, and reuse encoder-side linear projections so the reducer adds sub-millisecond overhead on typical resolutions. The entire pipeline is architecture-agnostic for encoder outputs that present token embeddings, keys/values, and positions; it requires no retraining and can be toggled on at inference.
D.rationale:
Inference time in VLMs grows with the number of visual tokens, yet many tokens are redundant background or near-duplicates. By explicitly measuring intra-image uniqueness (to avoid paying multiple times for the same content) and prompt-conditioned relevance (to keep only what the question likely needs), we can safely prune and merge tokens without changing the encoder. Prototype similarity further filters common backgrounds that seldom help downstream reasoning. Merging low-importance tokens into supertokens preserves aggregate evidence in the exact form cross-attention consumes (keys/values), maintaining answer quality while cutting compute.

The optional pseudo-relevance feedback mirrors information retrieval practice: a quick, cheap first look provides cues about what matters, then a second pass focuses the visual budget there. An adaptive EMA-calibrated threshold keeps the reducer robust under varying scenes and prompts, maintaining a desired latency-accuracy trade-off. Together, these pieces deliver substantial latency reductions with minimal regression and zero encoder retraining.

These are real gaps but none are blocking contradictions — an ML practitioner could fill them with reasonable design choices.

query_text:
Inputs to generative models vary in complexity but standard
decoding allocates fixed compute per token; how can a model
adaptively allocate more or fewer computation steps without
degrading quality?

A.method:
We propose Compute-Adaptive Decoding (CAD), an augmentation to an autoregressive generator that allocates per-token computation via a learned halting controller and a selective refinement branch. Each decoding step is decomposed into M "micro-steps" (e.g., blocks or recurrent updates that reuse the KV-cache). After each micro-step, a small controller head reads the current hidden state and a few cheap diagnostics (logit entropy, top-2 margin, change-in-logits norm across the last two micro-steps, and agreement with a lightweight auxiliary head) and outputs (a) a halting probability estimating the expected gain from continuing computation and (b) a hardness score that can trigger additional refinement actions. Decoding for a token halts as soon as the controller’s calibrated expected-gain falls below a threshold or a per-token cap is reached; when marked hard, the token can receive extra depth, wider expert activation, or a brief deliberation pass before halting.

Training proceeds in two phases. First, with the base model frozen, we construct oracle-gain targets by running teacher-forced passes that reveal the marginal improvement in token-level loss (or log-prob on the ground-truth token) from taking an additional micro-step, up to a small maximum M. We label micro-steps as beneficial when the measured gain exceeds a tolerance and train the controller to predict this signal with a binary cross-entropy/regression loss, plus a calibration loss on its uncertainty diagnostics. Second, we jointly fine-tune the base model and controller under a compute-regularized objective: token NLL + λ × expected-ponder-cost − α × distillation-to-full-compute when halting early. We use a straight-through or Gumbel-sigmoid estimator for discrete halts, enforce a global compute budget via a Lagrangian multiplier that adapts λ during training, and temperature-scale the controller to be well-calibrated on a held-out set.

For tokens predicted hard, CAD unlocks a selective refinement branch rather than defaulting to uniform heavy compute. Three knobs are available, and the controller chooses among them under a per-token budget: (1) deeper micro-steps (continue through more blocks), (2) wider compute by activating more experts in a sparse MoE layer for the next micro-step, and/or (3) a light deliberation pass that samples k low-temperature candidate tokens, scores them with a verifier that estimates one-step downstream quality (e.g., predicts the next-step loss or contradiction risk for each candidate using a compact scorer), and selects the best. This branch only activates when the predicted gain justifies it and is tightly budgeted (small k, shallow verifier), so average FLOPs/token remain low while difficult tokens get targeted help.

Inference exposes two user knobs: a quality floor and a compute cap. The controller is calibrated so that it never halts early when its expected improvement exceeds a threshold chosen to match or exceed baseline quality; in ambiguous cases, it falls back to the full-compute path. The algorithm is cache-efficient (micro-steps reuse KV states) and streaming-friendly. Evaluation measures include perplexity, edit distance to full-compute outputs, factuality/consistency scores on long-form tasks, and FLOPs/token. Ablations compare: fixed-compute baseline, CAD without refinement, CAD with each refinement option, and varying budgets; we expect CAD to reduce average compute substantially while preserving or improving quality, with the largest savings on easy tokens.
A.rationale:
Token difficulty is highly non-uniform: many positions are predictable (large logit margins) while a minority drive most errors. A learned halting controller tied to uncertainty diagnostics and trained against oracle gains lets the model stop early when additional depth has negligible marginal value, and spend extra steps only where they are expected to reduce loss. Distillation-to-full-compute and calibrated thresholds provide a safety net so that early exits occur only when they preserve the teacher’s behavior, preventing quality regressions.

Selective refinement concentrates expensive operations on just the hard cases: activating more depth/experts increases capacity precisely when needed, and a tiny verifier-guided deliberation is an efficient substitute for wide beam search. The compute-regularized training with a budgeted Lagrangian drives the system to meet a target FLOPs/token while the expected-gain rule maintains or improves accuracy, yielding adaptive computation without degrading quality.

novelty.reasoning: The literature search surfaces several related works: per-token early exit frameworks (FREE, TIDE, AdaPonderLM, ToEx, QuickSilver), oracle-based routing for adaptive computation (Duo-LLM), and ACT-style halting in recurrent transformers. However, none of these papers combine all the distinctive elements of CAD: (1) oracle-gain targets derived from marginal token-level loss improvement across micro-steps to train a halting controller, (2) a multi-knob selective refinement branch that chooses among deeper micro-steps, MoE expert widening, and verifier-guided deliberation (sampling k candidates scored by a compact downstream-quality verifier), and (3) compute-regularized joint fine-tuning with a Lagrangian budget constraint plus distillation-to-full-compute safety net. AdaPonderLM is the closest match (ACT-style halting, KV reuse, self-supervised difficulty allocation) but lacks the oracle-gain training signal and the multi-knob refinement branch. Duo-LLM uses oracle routing patterns but doesn't combine this with the deliberation/verifier branch or the Lagrangian training. B.method:
We propose Adaptive Compute Decoding (ACD), a per-token scheduler that decides, at each generation step, how much computation to spend and which decoding strategy to invoke. ACD maintains a lightweight difficulty score d_t built from readily available signals (logit entropy and top-1 vs top-2 margin) and a learned value head ψ_t that predicts the marginal benefit of “thinking more” before committing a token. ψ_t is trained via self-sampled trajectories with step-level scoring, so d_t reflects both local uncertainty and the expected downstream gain from extra search. Based on d_t, ACD routes each step into one of three regimes: Easy (speculate-and-verify), Medium (near-greedy decoding with tiny reranking), or Hard (focused multi-branch lookahead). A conservative acceptance policy guarantees we never underperform a strong baseline: if extra computation fails to clear a quality margin, we fall back to the baseline token.

Easy regime: When d_t is low, ACD uses verification-preserving speculative decoding. It drafts multiple next tokens at once using dense-retrieval proposals keyed by the model’s current hidden state, filters them to agree with the base model’s own next-token prediction, and then batch-verifies the draft against the base model. Accepted runs are committed exactly, so outputs are indistinguishable from standard decoding while amortizing many steps into one verification pass. In parallel, a small predictor gates intermediate-FFN activations for these easy steps so the forward pass itself is sparse, further reducing FLOPs without changing outputs.

Medium regime: For moderate d_t, ACD applies the most reliable low-overhead decoding for the task type (e.g., greedy or small-contrastive on closed-ended tasks; low-temperature sampling on open-ended ones), optionally with a micro-beam or tiny reranker to catch near ties. This regime stays near greedy latency and is invoked only when speculative acceptance is likely to shorten, ensuring stable quality without incurring significant overhead.

Hard regime: When d_t is high, ACD allocates extra reasoning. It performs a short, focused multi-branch lookahead guided by ψ_t as a twist/value function to favor promising continuations. ACD samples a small set of candidate continuations, resamples toward high-ψ prefixes, and scores step-wise with a process reward model to detect when additional search is still paying off. The token to commit is chosen by reweighted model probability or verifier-approved utility; if none beats the baseline by a learned margin, ACD halts search and outputs the baseline token, capping worst-case degradation at zero.

Training and calibration: We self-generate trees of solutions and learn (i) a process reward model that provides step scores and dynamic value margins; (ii) the ψ_t head to approximate expected future gain; (iii) a predictor that gates FFN activations. We also build the dense-retrieval datastore for speculation offline and tune routing thresholds {τ_easy, τ_hard} on a validation set to meet latency targets. At runtime, ACD adapts further by monitoring speculative acceptance rates and search gains to up- or down-shift budgets within a fixed end-to-end latency envelope.
B.rationale:
ACD matches computation to difficulty: easy tokens are cheaply generated via speculate-and-verify and sparse forwards, medium tokens use near-greedy decoding that is empirically robust, and only hard tokens trigger deeper search focused by a learned value signal. Because speculative steps are verified against the base model and hard-search commits only when a quality margin is met (else falls back), ACD preserves or improves quality relative to a strong baseline while reducing average latency.

The value-driven guidance concentrates expensive exploration where it matters, akin to resampling in sequential Monte Carlo, and the process reward model provides a calibrated halting signal that avoids both premature commitment and wasted thinking. Empirical decoding guidance ensures the mid-regime remains efficient and stable across task types, and predictor-guided sparsity cuts FLOPs on easy steps without altering the model’s distribution. Together, these components deliver adaptive per-token compute without quality regression.

C.method:
We propose Adaptive Token Compute with Saturation Exits and Masked Refinement (ATC-SMR), a decoding-time controller that assigns variable compute per token while preserving quality. The base generator is augmented with lightweight heads after several decoder layers (multi-exits). For each token being decoded, we compute a saturation signal as the cosine similarity between consecutive layer hidden states and a confidence signal from the token’s predictive entropy and shallow–deep head agreement. If saturation is high and confidence is sufficient, we early-exit at that layer; otherwise we continue deeper. Thresholds are mildly decayed over the generation horizon to accommodate growing context, and are calibrated to keep the model’s predicted risk below a target bound.

When a token fails either saturation or confidence checks at the deepest layer, we trigger a masked refinement loop instead of blindly spending full-context compute. A small Explainer MLP predicts a sparse importance mask over the already-generated context tokens for the current output position. We then run one or more refinement steps that re-attend only to the top-k masked context tokens using a compact residual block (shared across steps) and update the token’s logits. Each refinement step is itself guarded by an inner saturation test; if the token’s state saturates or confidence becomes high, we stop; otherwise we permit up to R refinements before falling back to a single full-context pass as a safety net.

For longer outputs, ATC-SMR also supports chunkwise iterative verification to avoid unnecessary recomputation. We decode the next K-token draft with shallow exits (or a small draft sub-network), cache these tokens, and then run a few Picard-style sweeps: on each sweep we selectively re-evaluate only a subset of positions in the window (e.g., those with the lowest confidence) with the main model while conditioning on cached tokens elsewhere. Tokens whose states saturate are finalized and dropped from subsequent sweeps; uncertain tokens receive additional masked refinement. This lets easy tokens finalize after very shallow computation while hard tokens get extra passes without reprocessing the entire window repeatedly.

Training pairs the standard LM loss with three auxiliaries that make adaptive compute reliable: (1) exit distillation that encourages shallow exits to match the deepest head, improving shallow accuracy and calibration; (2) a saturation-aware risk head that predicts whether a shallow exit would violate a target error budget, which we use to set thresholds at inference; and (3) mask learning via a factual/counterfactual objective on token representations to ensure the Explainer MLP highlights causally-relevant context tokens. Optionally, for stability of second-pass corrections, we use a sampler that mixes in ground-truth embeddings at mispredicted positions during training of the refinement module, improving convergence and preventing error amplification.

At inference, the scheduler is simple: attempt a shallow exit; if not safe, go deeper; if still uncertain, run up to R masked refinements; if still uncertain, perform one full-context pass; and, for chunked decoding, apply a small number of selective verification sweeps. All decisions are local and parallelizable across beam/sequence positions when applicable, and each stage has a conservative fallback to maintain output quality while still realizing significant average compute savings.
C.rationale:
ATC-SMR adapts compute to per-token difficulty by combining proven early-exit signals (hidden-state saturation) with uncertainty-aware refinement that focuses extra attention only where it matters. Easy tokens saturate quickly and exit shallowly; hard tokens receive additional, targeted computation via masked refinement and, in long outputs, limited iterative verification—avoiding full recomputation of the entire context. Because each stage is gated by confidence and offers a conservative fallback to full-context evaluation, quality is preserved while average latency and FLOPs drop.

The design choices directly address the pitfalls of naive adaptive compute: similarity-based exits give a robust, model-internal readiness signal; uncertainty-guided Top-K masking concentrates extra work on influential context instead of the whole prefix; and Picard-style chunked sweeps enable iterative correction without serial bottlenecks. Auxiliary training—exit distillation, calibrated risk prediction, and causal mask learning—makes the controller trustworthy, ensuring that adaptive decisions rarely harm quality.

D.method:
We propose Token-Adaptive Pondering (TAP), a lightweight wrapper around any autoregressive decoder that allocates variable compute per token with a no-regret guard. At each step t, the base model produces its next-token distribution and a small difficulty head predicts an uncertainty u_t and a predicted marginal utility of extra compute Δ_t. A controller maps these to a halting probability and a compute budget k_t ∈ {0, …, Kmax}. If k_t = 0, we accept the base token; if k_t > 0, we invoke a local refinement loop that spends up to k_t additional micro-steps to improve that decision. A no-regret criterion ensures quality never drops: if refinement fails to yield a higher verifier score than the baseline token, we keep the baseline.

The difficulty head is a single-pass, low-cost module attached to the decoder’s pre-logit representation that estimates token-level uncertainty via discriminative prototypes and outputs a calibrated probability and Δ_t. To avoid chattering on noisy signals, we smooth u_t with a tiny Kalman-style update that fuses the current estimate with a momentum-like prior over difficulty across timesteps, with online noise adaptation. The controller uses the smoothed uncertainty and surprisal (entropy of the base distribution) to choose k_t and an early-exit threshold for the refinement loop.

Refinement executes a micro-decoding loop that can be implemented in two interchangeable ways: (1) depth-on-demand, which iterates a small recurrent refinement block over the token’s hidden state (akin to taking additional integration steps when the signal is rapidly changing), or (2) candidate self-consistency, which proposes m alternative continuations from the base distribution and selects among them. Selection uses a fast pairwise preference scorer (shared with the base model) and/or rescoring under a frozen verifier; we stop early if marginal gains fall below a threshold. This inner loop is event-driven: when surprisal/uncertainty are high, the hidden-state update takes more micro-steps; when they are low, it halts immediately.

Training mixes three losses: the standard next-token NLL, a compute-regularization term that encourages small expected k_t while allowing high k_t where it raises likelihood, and a pairwise ranking loss that teaches the selector/verifier to prefer refinements that improve downstream metrics. We calibrate the uncertainty head with a held-out set and temperature scaling, and distill the verifier from a stronger model or task metric proxy. At inference, we bucket positions across the batch by k_t to run extra compute only for the hard tokens, keeping throughput high while preserving or improving quality via the no-regret guard.
D.rationale:
Token difficulty varies widely across contexts; allocating identical depth or sampling effort to every position wastes compute on easy tokens and starves hard ones. TAP targets compute where uncertainty and surprisal indicate likely gains, while a smoothed controller prevents overreacting to minibatch noise. The refinement loop is local and anytime: it halts as soon as improvements stabilize, and the no-regret guard ensures quality never drops below the baseline decode.

By separating a cheap, calibrated selector from the heavier refinement and using pairwise/verifier-based selection rather than self-judgment alone, TAP avoids the common failure mode where a model’s own confidence misleads its allocation. Event-driven micro-steps on the hidden state provide fine-grained adaptation akin to variable-step integration—more inner computation only when the token’s dynamics warrant it—yielding large wall-clock savings without quality degradation.
